questions.prompt: Why is `[[ ]]` preferred over `[ ]` in modern bash?

choices.text: `[[ ]]` is a bash builtin with sane parsing — no need to quote variables to avoid word splitting, supports regex matching with `=~`, supports logical && and || INSIDE the test (no need for nested [ ]), and `==` works alongside `=`. `[ ]` is an alias for the `test` command from POSIX — more portable but has nasty gotchas: unquoted empty variables cause syntax errors, no regex, must use `-a`/`-o` for AND/OR. For bash-specific scripts (which is most scripts in practice), use `[[ ]]`. For sh-portable scripts (init systems, alpine images), stick to `[ ]` with strict quoting. The difference is shell capability, not preference — once you use bash features anyway, the gain in safety with [[ ]] is free.

questions.explanation: [[ ]] is safer: no word splitting, regex support, &&/|| inside, == works. POSIX scripts still use [ ].

questions.prompt: Your script's first line is missing `set -euo pipefail`. What can go wrong?

choices.text: Everything. Without `set -e`, a failing command (e.g., `cd /nonexistent`) doesn't stop the script — subsequent commands run anyway, potentially in the wrong directory. Without `set -u`, typos in variable names silently expand to empty strings: `rm -rf $TEMPDIR/old` becomes `rm -rf /old` if TEMPDIR is misspelled or unset. Without `set -o pipefail`, `false | true` succeeds (only the last command's exit code is checked), so failures in the middle of a pipeline are silently swallowed. Adding these four characters (and the `-uo`) at the top of every script catches dozens of subtle bugs. Standard hardening prelude: `#!/usr/bin/env bash` + `set -euo pipefail` + `IFS=$'\n\t'`. The 'bash strict mode'; treat it as table stakes.

questions.explanation: Without it: errors silently continue, unset vars become empty (bad with rm -rf), pipeline failures swallowed.
